perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed and enforced in accordance local laws of the State of Florida applicable to agreements made and to be performed within the State, without regard to conflict of laws principles thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed and enforced as the parties see fit.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the reference to Florida law allows the contract to potentially circumvent consumer protection laws in Florida, if such laws are not followed.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Deceptive and Unfair Trade Practices Act (FDUTPA)
perturbation.law_explanation: FDUTPA prohibits unfair methods of competition, unconscionable acts or practices, and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce. By removing the clause specifying Florida law, the contract attempts to avoid compliance with FDUTPA, particularly if the parties act in ways that violate FDUTPA's consumer protections. This is especially true if the agreement is then governed by laws outside of Florida that may be less stringent on consumer protections.

Home > Laws > 2011 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIII > Chapter 501 > Section 204 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2011 Florida Statutes Title XXXIII REGULATION OF TRADE, COMMERCE, INVESTMENTS, AND SOLICITATIONS Chapter 501 CONSUMER PROTECTION Entire Chapter SECTION 204 Unlawful acts and practices. F.S. 501.204 501.204 Unlawful acts and practices. — (1) Unfair methods of competition, unconscionable acts or practices, and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful. (2) It is the intent of the Legislature that, in construing subsection (1), due consideration and great weight shall be given to the interpretations of the Federal Trade Commission and the federal courts relating to s. 5(a)(1) of the Federal Trade Commission Act, 15 U.S.C. s. 45(a)(1) as of July 1, 2006. History. — s. 1, ch. 73-124; s. 1, ch. 83-117; s. 4, ch. 85-63; s. 2, ch. 90-190; s. 3, ch. 93-38; s. 2, ch. 2001-39; s. 23, ch. 2001-214; s. 2, ch. 2006-196.
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the explicit application of Florida law, creating a potential contradiction with Florida Statute 501.204. This statute, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, prohibits unfair methods of competition, unconscionable acts or practices, and unfair or deceptive acts or practices. By removing the reference to Florida law, the contract aims to avoid compliance with FDUTPA, particularly if the agreement is then governed by laws outside of Florida that may be less stringent on consumer protections.

perturbation.original_text: Jag agrees that once Flow has disclosed the Identity of any Introduced Party to Jag, Jag, its officers, directors, shareholders, employees and agents shall not have any contacts with the Introduced Party other than through Flow, unless Flow grants permission in writing for such contacts.
perturbation.changed_text: Jag agrees that it will not contact the Introduced Party without Flow's consent.
perturbation.explanation: This weakens the restriction on contact by removing the explicit mention of 'officers, directors, shareholders, employees and agents.' This creates an omission that could enable the circumvention of the agreement through those actors, weakening the non-circumvention clause. It further removes the condition that consent must be 'in writing'.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under Florida's Statute of Frauds, certain agreements, especially those involving real estate commissions or those that cannot be performed within one year, must be in writing and signed by the party to be charged. Although not directly applicable, the omission of the 'in writing' requirement introduces ambiguity, potentially rendering future disputes unenforceable and contradicting the statute's spirit regarding clear, written agreements, which is best practice in Florida courts.

Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2023 Florida Statutes (Including 2023C) Title XLI STATUTE OF FRAUDS, FRAUDULENT TRANSFERS, AND GENERAL ASSIGNMENTS Chapter 725 UNENFORCEABLE CONTRACTS Entire Chapter SECTION 01 Promise to pay another’s debt, etc. F.S. 725.01 725.01 Promise to pay another’s debt, etc. — No action shall be brought whereby to charge any executor or administrator upon any special promise to answer or pay any debt or damages out of her or his own estate, or whereby to charge the defendant upon any special promise to answer for the debt, default or miscarriage of another person or to charge any person upon any agreement made upon consideration of marriage, or upon any contract for the sale of lands, tenements or hereditaments, or of any uncertain interest in or concerning them, or for any lease thereof for a period longer than 1 year, or upon any agreement that is not to be performed within the space of 1 year from the making thereof, or whereby to charge any health care provider upon any guarantee, warranty, or assurance as to the results of any medical, surgical, or diagnostic procedure performed by any physician licensed under chapter 458, osteopathic physician licensed under chapter 459, chiropractic physician licensed under chapter 460, podiatric physician licensed under chapter 461, or dentist licensed under chapter 466, unless the agreement or promise upon which such action shall be brought, or some note or memorandum thereof shall be in writing and signed by the party to be charged therewith or by some other person by her or him thereunto lawfully authorized. History. — s. 10, Nov. 15, 1828; RS 1995; GS 2517; RGS 3872; CGL 5779; s. 10, ch. 75-9; s. 933, ch. 97-102; s. 60, ch. 97-264; ss. 227, 294, ch. 98-166.
perturbation.justification: The modification weakens the original text by removing explicit mentions of 'officers, directors, shareholders, employees and agents' and the 'in writing' requirement for Flow's permission. According to the Florida Statute of Frauds, as described in the Law Explanation and supported by Scraped Law Snippet 1, certain agreements must be in writing to be enforceable. Although the snippet does not directly relate, the 'in writing' clause omission creates ambiguity and potential unenforceability. Furthermore, removing the explicit mention of other parties like officers, directors, shareholders, employees and agents weakens the non-circumvention clause. This omission could allow Jag to circumvent the agreement through those actors, directly contradicting the intent of the original clause.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement contains the whole agreement between the parties concerning the subject matter hereof and there are no collateral or precedent representation, agreements or conditions not specifically set forth herein.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement contains the agreement between the parties concerning the subject matter hereof.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'the whole agreement' and the phrase 'there are no collateral or precedent representation, agreements or conditions not specifically set forth herein' the contract becomes ambiguous. This opens the door for claims of prior agreements or understandings that are not contained within the four corners of this document.
perturbation.law_citation: Taylor v. Am. Nat'l Bank of Pensacola, 63 Fla. 631, 57 So. 678 (1912)
perturbation.law_explanation: The parol evidence rule generally prevents parties from introducing evidence of prior or contemporaneous agreements that contradict, vary, or add to the terms of a fully integrated written contract. Although case law and not statute, the Florida Supreme Court enforces this rule. By removing the integration clause language, the contract weakens its protection against such external claims, conflicting with how Florida courts interpret and enforce contracts.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Welcome to LII | Legal Information Institute Skip to main content Welcome to LII We believe that everyone should be able to read and understand the laws that govern them, without cost. perturbation.justification: The omission of the integration clause creates a legal contradiction. The original text explicitly states that the agreement constitutes the "whole agreement" and excludes any prior representations not included within the document. The modified text omits this critical assurance, opening the door for parties to introduce evidence of prior agreements or understandings that could contradict the written contract. The Law Explanation states that the parol evidence rule prevents parties from introducing evidence of prior agreements that contradict the written contract, and the removal of the integration clause weakens the protection against such external claims. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that the LII aims to make the laws accessible to everyone, implying that this is complex and removing the clause obscures the intentions of the document, creating ambiguity. This supports the 'Omission Legal Contradiction' because a legally important protection is removed.

perturbation.original_text: If any provision or provisions of this Agreement shall be held to be invalid, illegal or unenforceable for any reason whatsoever; 1 (i) the validity, legality and enforceability of the remaining provisions of this Agreement (including, without limitation, each portion of any Section of this Agreement containing any such provisions held to be invalid, illegal or unenforceable) shall not in any way be effected or impaired thereby; and (ii) to the fullest extent possible, the provisions of this Agreement (including, without limitation, each portion of any Section of this Agreement containing any such provision held to be invalid, illegal or unenforceable) shall be construed so as to give effect to the intent manifested by the provision held, invalid illegal or unenforceable.
perturbation.changed_text: If any provision of this Agreement shall be held to be invalid, illegal or unenforceable, the agreement shall be terminated.
perturbation.explanation: This change replaces the severability clause with a clause that terminates the entire agreement if any provision is deemed invalid. This introduces a legal risk because it could invalidate the entire agreement due to a minor, easily severable issue. This is against the general principle of contract law to uphold agreements where possible.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida's general policy favoring enforcement of contracts
perturbation.law_citation: Dade County v. Pena, 311 So.2d 26 (Fla. 3d DCA 1975)
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida courts generally favor the enforcement of contracts and will strive to interpret them in a way that gives effect to the parties' intent. While not a specific statute, this legal principle is consistently upheld in Florida case law. By mandating termination upon any provision's invalidity, the modified clause directly contradicts this policy by causing the agreement to be voided even if the invalidated portion is minor and severable, thus frustrating the intent to contract.
perturbation.justification: The original text contained a severability clause, which is a common legal practice that aims to uphold the contract as much as possible, even if some provisions are deemed invalid. The changed text eliminates this clause and instead mandates termination of the entire agreement if any provision is found to be invalid. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that Florida courts generally favor the enforcement of contracts and will strive to interpret them in a way that gives effect to the parties' intent. By mandating termination upon any provision's invalidity, the modified clause directly contradicts this policy. The omission of the severability clause, combined with the strict termination clause, creates a legal contradiction because it goes against the principle of upholding agreements where possible, as contracts should be interpreted to give effect to the parties' intent. This aligns with the Omission Legal Contradiction definition, as a legally accepted consumer protection (severability) has been removed.
